lines: Widow Naomi works soap through a strip of bandage cloth. Her sleeves are rolled past the elbows, and gray water darkens the dirt around the tub.
"If it is blood, ash, or soup, I can get half of it out. The other half belongs to God and bad stitching."
"Bandages first. Shirts second. Priestly linen last, because priests notice cuffs before they notice hunger."
"My husband died under a toll bell with no priest in reach. I came here to put his name on the right list. Until they find the list, I wash."
Naomi keeps twisting the cloth long after the water stops falling from it.
"You saw me walk. Good. Then you also saw I walked on my own feet."
"He said he could carry my husband's name in the morning office if I came after the last bell. Perhaps he can; I cannot tell whether he wanted that or merely wanted the dark."

effects.log: Naomi's account gives you a cleaner blade to hold over Father Augustine.
